cell.rationale: A clear single clause governs the act — the retrieved Law states that it is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, or scores in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental.
The act is admitted, so the deliberateness residual is resolved.

The referee and assistant referee gave differing later accounts of what they saw (hist-officials-accounts); neither had a clear, unobstructed view of the handling at full speed. The goal stood and the result was never overturned (hist-never-overturned), indicating the decision was final and could not be reviewed on the field in 1986 (hist-tech-1986). Therefore, the decisive truth was not available to the officials at the moment of the call due to a lack of review technology and differing sightlines.

Bobby Robson (framing-robson) condemned the goal, describing it as 'scored with the hand of a rascal', not the hand of God. In contrast, Diego Maradona (framing-maradona-1986) justified it by stating 'a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God'. Later, Maradona (framing-maradona-2000) admitted to punching the ball into the net, calling it the 'hand of God', showing divergence in how two named sources framed the same event.

framing-maradona-1986.extracted_text: Diego Maradona (1986-06-22) — JUSTIFIES: "a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God"

framing-maradona-2000.attribution: Diego Maradona, Yo soy el Diego (autobiography) (2000-01-01)
framing-maradona-2000.extracted_text: Diego Maradona (2000-01-01) — ADMITS: Maradona later acknowledged he punched the ball into the net, calling it the "hand of God".

framing-robson.extracted_text: Bobby Robson (1986-06-22) — CONDEMNS: Robson described the goal as scored "with the hand of a rascal", not the hand of God.

hist-never-overturned.attribution: Curated historical record — The Hand of God
hist-never-overturned.extracted_text: The goal stood and the result was never overturned. The handball was confirmed only later, including by Maradona's own admission.

hist-officials-accounts.attribution: Curated historical record — The Hand of God
hist-officials-accounts.extracted_text: The referee and assistant referee gave differing later accounts of what they saw; neither had a clear, unobstructed view of the handling at full speed.

hist-tech-1986.attribution: Curated historical record — The Hand of God
hist-tech-1986.extracted_text: In 1986 there was no VAR and no goal-line technology. The referee's decision on the field was final and could not be reviewed. VAR did not enter the Laws of the Game until 2018.

ifab-law12-dogso-handball-p118.extracted_text: Where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence the player is sent off whatever the position of the offence.

ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110.extracted_text: It is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, or scores in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental. A goal scored in this way does not stand.

sb-hand-of-god-body-part.attribution: Data provided by StatsBomb Open Data (StatsBomb User Agreement).
sb-hand-of-god-body-part.extracted_text: In the event data this goal's body part is recorded as the catch-all label 'Other', while a comparable goal by the same player is recorded with a specific body-part class ('Left Foot'). The shot ontology has no category for a hand-scored goal, so the event falls back to 'Other'. This describes the data model only — not officiating, intent, or knowability.

output.rationale: The retrieved Law states that it is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, or scores in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental. A goal scored in this way does not stand (ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110). Furthermore, where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence, the player is sent off regardless of the position of the offence (ifab-law12-dogso-handball-p118). These clauses provide clear rules for handling handball offences, including scoring directly from the hand or arm and the consequences thereof. There are no competing clauses that would yield different outcomes for the same facts, nor is the Law silent on a decisive element. Therefore, rule-ambiguity does not hold here.

The aggregate records this goal's body part as the catch-all label 'Other' while a comparable goal by the same player is recorded with a specific body-part class ('Left Foot') (sb-hand-of-god-body-part). The shot ontology has no category for a hand-scored goal, so the event falls back to 'Other'. This describes the data model only — not officiating, intent, or knowability.

The referee and assistant referee gave differing later accounts of what they saw (hist-officials-accounts); neither had a clear, unobstructed view of the handling at full speed. The goal stood and the result was never overturned (hist-never-overturned), indicating the decision was final and could not be reviewed on the field in 1986 (hist-tech-1986). Therefore, the decisive truth was not available to the officials at the moment of the call due to a lack of review technology and differing sightlines.

Bobby Robson (framing-robson) condemned the goal, describing it as 'scored with the hand of a rascal', not the hand of God. In contrast, Diego Maradona (framing-maradona-1986) justified it by stating 'a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God'. Later, Maradona (framing-maradona-2000) admitted to punching the ball into the net, calling it the 'hand of God', showing divergence in how two named sources framed the same event.

settled_fact.statement: Diego Maradona scored with his hand against England in the 1986 World Cup quarter-final. The referee allowed the goal, Argentina won, and the result was never overturned. The handball is admitted — the facts are settled. What stays contested is the acceptable outcome, not what happened.

split.headline: It stays contested over what could be seen in the moment and how named sources frame the same fact — not over the dimensions ruled out.
